title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
There’s a term making the rounds: vibe coding. Coined by Andrej Karpathy, it describes the new mode of AI-assisted development where you describe what you want in natural language, accept the generated code with minimal review, and “forget that the code even exists.”
It’s a catchy label for something real. Tools like Claude Code, GitHub Copilot’s coding agent, and Cursor are genuinely changing how software gets written. But they’ve also quietly removed something that used to matter: the moment when a human read the code and asked, is this actually what I meant?

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
The Problem Isn’t Code Quality
The naive worry about AI-generated code is that it’ll be wrong in obvious ways — bugs, hallucinated APIs, logic errors. And that does happen. But the deeper problem is more subtle.
LLM-generated code is plausible by construction. It compiles. It passes the tests you wrote. It looks like idiomatic Python or Rust or Go. The surface-level quality is often impressive.
What it doesn’t do is know what you meant.
Consider a deceptively simple example: “given a list of integers, remove duplicates.” There are at least two natural interpretations:

Keep one copy of each element that appears more than once: [1,2,3,2,4] → [1,2,3,4]
Remove all elements that appear more than once: [1,2,3,2,4] → [1,3,4]

A typical LLM will produce interpretation 1 without hesitation — because it’s statistically more common in training data. But if you wanted interpretation 2, you now have a bug that looks like working code. It’ll pass most tests you’d think to write. It might ship.
This is the intent gap: the semantic distance between what you meant and what the program does.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
Why Vibe Coding Amplifies It
The intent gap isn’t new. It’s always existed in software engineering. What AI changes is the scale and the invisibility.
Traditional development had natural checkpoints: you wrote the code, you re-read it, a reviewer read it, you debugged it step by step. Each of these was an opportunity to notice that the implementation diverged from the intent.
In the vibe coding workflow, those checkpoints are being bypassed. You type a prompt, accept a diff, and move on. The ratio of code produced to code carefully examined is growing rapidly. AI writes faster than humans can review.
And crucially: AI-generated bugs are harder to spot than hand-written ones, precisely because the code looks right. A human programmer’s mistake often has a “this looks weird” quality. AI-generated misinterpretations are fluent and stylistically impeccable.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
Specifications as the Answer
The standard response to software bugs is testing. Write more tests, better tests, edge case tests. This is good advice but it’s not sufficient here.
Tests check specific inputs. They can’t tell you whether the intent was correctly captured — they can only tell you whether the code matches the tests. And your tests were probably written based on the same informal prompt that generated the code. They’ll share the same blind spots.
What’s needed is something more fundamental: formal specifications. A specification describes what the code should do in a way that can be mechanically checked — not just against the examples you thought to include, but against the space of all possible behaviors.
For the “remove duplicates” example, a formal postcondition makes the intent unambiguous:
# Intent: remove ALL elements that appear more than once
assert all(lst.count(x) == 1 for x in result)
assert all(x in result for x in lst if lst.count(x) == 1)
This is just a Python assertion, but it pins down the semantics precisely. Any implementation that satisfies it is correct; any that doesn’t is wrong. You can check it dynamically, use it for fuzz testing, or feed it to a verifier.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
A Spectrum of Formalism
One reason specifications have historically been unpopular is the perception that they’re all-or-nothing — either you do full formal verification (expensive, requires expertise) or you write tests (cheap, familiar).
But there’s actually a spectrum:

Targeted assertions at points of likely ambiguity — cheap, immediately useful, language-agnostic
Preconditions and postconditions on functions — more systematic, can be checked at runtime
Full functional specs in languages like Dafny or Verus — machine-checkable proofs of correctness
Domain-specific languages from which correct code is synthesized — the spec is the program

The key insight is that even partial specifications at the cheap end of the spectrum give substantial value. You don’t need to prove your whole codebase correct; you just need to pin down the parts where AI is most likely to misinterpret your intent.
Recent work from Microsoft Research (Lahiri et al., FSE 2024) shows that LLM-generated postconditions catch real bugs in production Java codebases — including bugs missed by classical invariant detectors. The same LLMs that create the intent gap can help close it, if you use them to generate specifications rather than just code.

title: The Intent Gap: Why AI-Generated Code Needs Specifications, Not Just Tests
text:
The Shift That’s Needed
The vibe coding moment has changed the role of the human in software development. We’ve moved from author to supervisor to — in the most extreme cases — passive consumer of AI-generated code.
This isn’t inherently bad. It’s a genuine productivity revolution. But it means the informal review process that used to catch intent gaps is weakened or gone.
The replacement has to be more systematic. Specifications — even lightweight ones — are the scalable mechanism for ensuring that AI-generated code does what you actually meant. Not because formal methods are suddenly easy, but because the alternative (trusting that the LLM guessed correctly) is getting less viable as the stakes get higher.
The question isn’t whether we need specifications. It’s how to make writing them cheap enough that people actually do it.
That’s an open problem worth working on.

Further reading: Lahiri et al., “Can Large Language Models Transform Natural Language Intent into Formal Method Postconditions?”, FSE 2024. The RiSE group at Microsoft Research has a nice blog post covering the broader research agenda.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
Consider the quadratic program on the simplex:
\max_{x \in \Delta_n}\; V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, x^\top Q\, x + q^\top x
where Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} is symmetric and \Delta_n = \{x \geq 0 : \mathbf{1}^\top x = 1\}.

If Q \preceq 0 (negative semidefinite), V is concave — gradient ascent finds the global maximum.
If Q \succeq 0 (positive semidefinite), V is convex — the maximum is at a vertex; check n+1 points.
If Q is indefinite, neither approach works. The problem is NP-hard in general (Motzkin–Straus: max clique reduces to \max x^\top Q x on \Delta_n).

This post describes a method that handles a large subclass of the indefinite case. A componentwise change of variables x_i = s_i(y_i) can render V concave in the new coordinates, and whether such a change exists is checkable by a semidefinite program with only n+1 constraints.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
Consider the quadratic program on the simplex:
\max_{x \in \Delta_n}\; V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\, x^\top Q\, x + q^\top x
where Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n} is symmetric and \Delta_n = \{x \geq 0 : \mathbf{1}^\top x = 1\}.

If Q \preceq 0 (negative semidefinite), V is concave — gradient ascent finds the global maximum.
If Q \succeq 0 (positive semidefinite), V is convex — the maximum is at a vertex; check n+1 points.
If Q is indefinite, neither approach works. The problem is NP-hard in general (Motzkin–Straus: max clique reduces to \max x^\top Q x on \Delta_n).

This post describes a method that handles a large subclass of the indefinite case. A componentwise change of variables x_i = s_i(y_i) can render V concave in the new coordinates, and whether such a change exists is checkable by a semidefinite program with only n+1 constraints.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
The reparameterization
We ask: is there a smooth, componentwise map x_i = s_i(y_i) that makes V(s(y)) concave in y?
If yes, then \max_y V(s(y)) is a concave maximization — solvable in polynomial time. The optimizer x^* = s(y^*) is the global maximum of V on \Delta_n.

The Hessian
For maps s_i with constant curvature \kappa_i = s_i''/(s_i')^2, the Hessian of V(s(y)) factors as:
H(y) = S'(y) \cdot M(x) \cdot S'(y)
where S' = \text{diag}(s_1', \ldots, s_n') is positive and:
M(x) = Q + \text{diag}\!\big(\kappa \circ \nabla V(x)\big), \qquad \nabla V(x) = Qx + q.
Since S' \succ 0, concavity of V(s(y)) is equivalent to M(x) \preceq 0 for all x \in \Delta_n.
The feasible set stays convex. For concave maps s_i (which is the case when \kappa_i < 0), the reparameterized feasible set \{y : s(y) \in \Delta_n\} = \{y \geq 0 : \sum_i s_i(y_i) = 1\} is convex: each s_i is concave, so \{y : \sum s_i(y_i) \leq 1\} is convex. The full reparameterized problem is therefore: maximize a concave function over a convex set — a standard convex program.


Reducing to a finite check
Two key facts:

M(x) is affine in x (because \nabla V is), hence matrix-concave.
A matrix-concave function on a polytope attains its maximum eigenvalue at a vertex.

The simplex \Delta_n has n + 1 vertices. So checking M(x) \preceq 0 everywhere reduces to:
\text{find}\; \kappa \;\;\text{s.t.}\;\; M(v_k) \preceq 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, n \tag{SDP}
This is a semidefinite program with n+1 matrix constraints of size n \times n — polynomial time.






Note



Why the simplex matters. On a box [0,1]^n, the same argument requires 2^n vertex checks. The simplex has only n+1.
Matrix-concavity is what matters. The vertex trick requires M(x) to be matrix-concave — meaning each diagonal entry M_{ii}(x) must be concave as a function of the full vector x, not just x_i. With constant \kappa_i, M_{ii}(x) = Q_{ii} + \kappa_i (Q_i^\top x + q_i) is affine in x and therefore concave. Allowing \kappa_i to depend on x_i makes M_{ii} quadratic in x, and the cross-term Hessian \partial^2 M_{ii}/\partial x_j \partial x_k = b_i(Q_{ij}\delta_{ik} + Q_{ik}\delta_{ij}) is indefinite whenever Q has off-diagonal entries. Finding reparameterization families that are richer than constant curvature while preserving matrix-concavity of M remains open.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
The tractable class

Definition
The tractable class \mathcal{T} is the set of (Q, q) pairs for which the SDP is feasible. It has a clean structure:

Convex in (Q, q) space — membership is efficiently testable.
Grows with \|q\| — as the linear term dominates, more problems become certifiable.
Excludes the provably hard cases — q = 0 gives the pure homogeneous QP \max x^\top Q x on \Delta_n, which encodes max clique. These are never certifiable.



The pipeline
For any (Q, q) \in \mathcal{T}:

Certify (poly time): solve the SDP to find curvature parameters \kappa.
Solve (poly time): maximize the now-concave V(s(y)) by gradient ascent.
Recover: x^* = s(y^*) is the global maximizer.



Limits
The method cannot certify every indefinite QP — only those where the linear term q is large enough relative to Q’s indefiniteness.
The boundary. As \|q\| \to 0, the feasible set shrinks: the diagonal boost \kappa \circ \nabla V(x) vanishes, and M(x) \to Q, which is indefinite by assumption. As \|q\| \to \infty, the feasible set grows: \nabla V \approx q at all vertices, providing a uniformly large diagonal correction.
What remains hard. Pure interaction games (q = 0), games where interactions dominate intrinsic payoffs (\|q\| \ll \|Q\|), and games with strongly conflicting off-diagonal structure that no diagonal correction can overcome.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
section: Application: welfare maximization in potential games
text:
Application: welfare maximization in potential games

Background
A potential game has a scalar function V(x) whose stationary points on \Delta_n are exactly the Nash equilibria. When V is quadratic, welfare maximization — finding the equilibrium with highest V — is exactly the simplex-constrained QP above.
This is NP-hard in general (Conitzer & Sandholm 2003, even inapproximable). For (Q, q) \in \mathcal{T}, the reparameterization solves it in polynomial time.


Example 1: traffic routing (vertex optimum)
A unit mass of commuters splits among n = 3 routes.









Route
Description
Free-flow time
Key feature




1 — Highway
Through city center
25 min
Fast but bottleneck-prone


2 — Bypass
Six-lane ring road
32 min
High capacity, long


3 — Local
Two-lane suburban road
28 min
Moderate; merges with both others




Q = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 & 0.5 \\ -1 & 0.5 & 1 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad q = \begin{pmatrix} 4.5 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}.

Q is indefinite (eigenvalues \approx +2.73, +0.54, -0.77). After coordinate elimination, the reduced matrix \tilde{Q} has \det = -3.25 — the indefiniteness is genuine.
Certification. With \kappa = -1.70, M(v) \prec 0 at all three vertices of the reduced simplex. The concavified V has a unique global maximum:
x^* = (1, 0, 0), \qquad V(x^*) = 5.5.
All commuters on the highway — the route with the best free-flow quality (q_1 = 4.5). Its self-congestion (Q_{11} = 2) is dominated by the intrinsic advantage. The complementarity Q_{12} = -1 with the bypass provides congestion relief, but the certificate tells us no mixed split improves on the pure highway. (See verify_routing_game.py for a self-contained verification script.)


Example 2: platform adoption (mixed optimum)

Q = \begin{pmatrix} -3 & 3.5 & 0.5 \\ 3.5 & 3.5 & 3 \\ 0.5 & 3 & 2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad q = \begin{pmatrix} 17 \\ 14 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix}.

Platforms 1 and 2 are synergistic (Q_{12} = 3.5) — adopting both creates positive network effects. Platform 1 has diminishing returns (Q_{11} = -3). The reduced matrix is indefinite (\det \tilde{Q} = -3).
Certification. With \kappa = -0.3, the NSD check passes. Gradient ascent converges to:
x^* \approx (0.46,\; 0.54,\; 0), \qquad V(x^*) = 16.44.
A mixed equilibrium — users split between the two synergistic platforms, nobody adopts platform 3. This optimum is not at any vertex and cannot be found by pure-strategy enumeration. The concavification certificate guarantees it is the unique global maximum.


Numerical evidence
On 500 random indefinite potential games (n = 4, \|q\|/\|Q\| \approx 3):




Count
%




Certifiably tractable
411
82%


Not certifiable
89
18%



Of the 411 certifiable games, 29 had interior global optima — fully mixed equilibria. The reparameterization found every one exactly, validated against brute-force global search.


Dynamics
In potential games, learning dynamics (replicator, gradient play) converge to some Nash equilibrium — but not necessarily the welfare-maximizing one. In the certifiable class, the reparameterized V is concave with a unique global maximum, so:






Note



Corollary. For games in the certifiable class, gradient-based dynamics converge to the welfare-maximizing equilibrium from almost all initial conditions.


Computational tractability and dynamical selection coincide.

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
Open questions
A diagonal dominance sufficient condition. The SDP is one way to certify the tractable class. A simpler (but more conservative) sufficient condition comes from diagonal dominance: M(x) \preceq 0 holds whenever each diagonal entry dominates its row, i.e., |\kappa_i| \cdot g_i(x) - Q_{ii} \geq \sum_{j \neq i} |Q_{ij}| for all i and x \in \Delta_n. Since g_i is affine and achieves its minimum at a vertex, this reduces to:
\min_{v \in \text{vert}(\Delta_n)} [Qv + q]_i \;>\; \sum_j |Q_{ij}| \cdot \frac{1}{|\kappa_i|} + Q_{ii}
In words: each coordinate’s minimum gradient (over vertices) must exceed the total absolute interaction strength of that coordinate. This is the “linear term dominates interactions” intuition made precise. It is not tight — the SDP exploits correlations between rows — but it gives a closed-form sufficient condition. Can this gap be characterized? Is there a scaled diagonal dominance condition (à la Geršgorin with optimal scaling) that is both closed-form and tight?
Richer reparameterizations. Constant-curvature maps give M(x) affine in x — hence matrix-concave, enabling the vertex trick. The natural generalization \kappa_i(x_i) = a_i + b_i x_i makes M_{ii} quadratic, but the Hessian \partial^2 M_{ii}/\partial x_j \partial x_k is indefinite whenever Q has off-diagonal entries, destroying matrix-concavity. Can a different parameterization of the maps — or a different proof strategy (e.g., SOS certificates) — extend the tractable class beyond constant curvature?
Relation to the S-lemma. The classical S-lemma establishes joint convexity of the numerical range of a pair of quadratic forms. Reparameterization convexifies the primal objective directly. Do these approaches certify the same class, or are they fundamentally different?

title: Convexification of Simplex-Constrained QPs, with an Application to Potential Games
text:
References

Monderer, D. & Shapley, L.S. (1996). Potential games. Games and Economic Behavior 14(1), 124–143.
Sandholm, W.H. (2010). Population Games and Evolutionary Dynamics. MIT Press.
Conitzer, V. & Sandholm, T. (2003). Complexity results about Nash equilibria. IJCAI, 765–771.
Bomze, I.M. (1998). On standard quadratic optimization problems. Journal of Global Optimization 13(4), 369–387.
Burer, S. & Ye, Y. (2020). Exactness of Parrilo’s conic approximations for copositive programs. Mathematics of Operations Research 45(1), 265–290.
